bibliographic_data.title: PropertyDAG: Multi-objective Bayesian optimization of partially ordered, mixed-variable properties for biological sequence design
bibliographic_data.abstract: Bayesian optimization offers a sample-efficient framework for navigating the exploration-exploitation trade-off in the vast design space of biological sequences. Whereas it is possible to optimize the various properties of interest jointly using a multi-objective acquisition function, such as the expected hypervolume improvement (EHVI), this approach does not account for objectives with a hierarchical dependency structure. We consider a common use case where some regions of the Pareto frontier are prioritized over others according to a specified partial ordering in the objectives. For instance, when designing antibodies, we maximize the binding affinity to a target antigen only if it can be expressed in live cell culture—modeling the experimental dependency in which affinity can only be measured for antibodies that can be expressed and thus produced in viable quantities. In general, we may want to confer a partial ordering to the properties such that each is optimized conditioned on its parent properties satisfying some feasibility condition. To this end, we present PropertyDAG, a framework that operates on top of the traditional multi-objective BO to impose this desired ordering on the objectives, e.g., expression → affinity. We demonstrate its performance over multiple simulated active learning iterations on a penicillin production task, toy numerical problem, and a real-world antibody design task.

core_content.novelty_claim: PropertyDAG introduces a framework that imposes a desired partial ordering on objectives in multi-objective Bayesian optimization, addressing hierarchical dependencies in biological sequence design.
core_content.key_methodology: PropertyDAG modifies the posterior inference procedure within standard BO by treating objectives as mixed-variable and transforming samples to conform to the specified partial ordering before entering the acquisition function.
outcomes: Identifies significantly more joint positive designs compared to standard BO
Trained surrogate models under Property-BO output more accurate predictions on joint positives in a held-out test set
core_content.full_text_markdown: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a popular technique for sample-efficient black-box optimization. In the context of designing biological sequences, traditional BO can optimize multiple properties jointly but does not account for hierarchical dependencies among objectives. PropertyDAG addresses this by imposing a desired partial ordering on objectives such as 'expression → affinity'. The framework modifies standard BO to handle mixed-variable objectives and ensures that samples conform to the specified order before entering the acquisition function. This approach is demonstrated through simulations on penicillin production, toy numerical problems, and real-world antibody design tasks, showing improved performance in identifying joint positive designs.
